summary_text:
S:
The patient reports a runny nose and stuffy congestion starting around supper time last night. He also reports pulling on his ears and experiencing intermittent irritability. He reports decreased appetite and reports feeling warm. He reports a rectal temperature of 100.3 degrees Fahrenheit and reports sweating. He reports a barky cough and dizziness. He reports using saline mist and Baby Vicks on his feet and Aveno shampoo on his cradle cap. He consumed approximately seven ounces from a bottle this morning and barely ate the offered cereal. He reports fighting to sleep. The patient's older sister has been sick, and he had a playdate with his nephew who had a sinus infection.

O:
Blood pressure is mildly low. Heart rate is within normal limits. Neurologic examination is nonfocal. Ears appear normal. The patient's cradle cap is worsening.

A:
The doctor believes the infection could become bacterial if left untreated. The patient suspects a viral infection. The doctor is watching for a barky cough.

P:
Order half a teaspoon of ibuprofen if a barky cough develops. Follow up in one week if symptoms do not improve. Recommend increased oral hydration and slow positional changes. Arrange follow-up if symptoms worsen or do not improve. Give the patient half a teaspoon of sister's medicine. Give the patient Tylenol this morning. Recommend using a soft brush and baby oil. Recommend using head & shoulder shampoo twice per week. Recommend using selsun blue shampoo twice per week. Give the patient saline mist.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Randy Gutierrez is a 9-month-old male who is being evaluated today for a cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. He is accompanied by his mother.

Symptoms began yesterday around dinnertime with clear nasal drainage, pulling on his ears bilaterally, and rubbing his nose frequently. He does not have a barky cough or a sore throat. His rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees. The patient did consume a 7 ounce bottle this morning, but mostly denied the next offered bottle and declined cereal. He is irritable and having trouble falling asleep, described “like he is fighting it”.

The patient has been exposed to an older sister who is sick and a cousin who has a sinus infection.

Treatment has included saline nasal spray, Tylenol, baby Vick's, and a humidifier. When the patient aroused this morning, he was warm to the touch. When his sleeper was removed, he was quite sweaty despite the air conditioner being on.

The patient's dandruff is also worsening. The only tried treatment is Aveeno shampoo.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Older sister.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Tylenol PRN.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Temperature 100.3 degrees.

Ears, Eyes, Nose, Throat
Nasal congestion, clear mucus, rhinorrhea. Bilateral pulling of ears, no sore throat or hoarseness.

Respiratory
Positive for cough.

ASSESSMENT

• Viral infection
• Dandruff

PLAN

Viral infection
The patient presents with a viral infection and does not need antibiotics currently. I advised continuation of fluids, rest. Additionally, I suggested trying Zarbee’s for children under 1 years old. If a croup cough develops, the patient will receive a 0.5 teaspoon.

I also provided counseling to the mother to avoid giving honey to the patient for his first year of life due to botulism spores.

Dandruff
The patient is experiencing worsening symptoms of dandruff. I recommended Head and Shoulders shampoo, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A-D 2 times per week, combined with his usual shampoo. I counseled the mother that she should be careful not to get the shampoo in the patient eyes and to not use the extra strength shampoos. Additionally, he may also use baby oil and a soft brush on the area.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return in one week for a re-check.

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient suspects a viral infection.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is the patient's own diagnostic impression/belief, which is subjective history and belongs in the Subjective section, not in the clinician Assessment.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor is watching for a barky cough.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This describes an explicit monitoring/management action (watching for a specific symptom), which is part of the Plan rather than the diagnostic Assessment.

section_miscategorizations.text: Give the patient half a teaspoon of sister's medicine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: As written, this appears to be a past or current home treatment (using a sibling's medicine), which is patient-reported medication use and belongs in Subjective; as a formal plan it would be inappropriate care, but structurally it should not be documented as a clinician plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: Give the patient Tylenol this morning.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This reads more like an already-administered medication during the visit (a completed intervention), which typically belongs in Objective/procedures performed rather than in the forward-looking Plan.

The Subjective section already notes that saline mist is being used at home; unless this is clearly a new order or change, it is better categorized as current home treatment rather than a new Plan item.
